title: Background
text: crucial step single cell/nucleus RNAseq analysis implementation clustering algorithms generate groups cells/nuclei similar gene expression patterns. clusters often necessary classify observations (cells/nuclei) based cell state (cell type, developmental stage, etc.) unleash potential single cell/ nucleus approach. Clusters generated based patterns expression subset features, selection features highly influential downstream results. selecting features dimension reduction clustering, genes mitochondrial genome, known sex-linked genes, cell cycle genes (depending dataset) often removed consideration. “biological quality controls” can help produce clusters representing desired cell state removing expression patterns linked donor sex cell size. Since canonical cell state marker genes highly expressed one subset observations lowly expressed absent others, common feature selection methods identify genes highly variable expression within dataset. methods often rely modeling mean-variance relationship allow consideration potential batch effects models. Batch consideration enables data-driven exclusion features capture technical noise biological differences relevant classification cell state.

title: Background
text: spatially resolved transcriptomics (SRT), individual observations single cells single nuclei, rather single points 2D space (tissue section). x- y-coordinates preserved sequencing, processed transcriptomic data can reconstruct gene expression patterns vary across different regions tissue present section. goal clustering SRT datasets often use gene expression patterns identify known spatial domains (e.g., different cortical layers brain). extra spatial dimension (x/y coordinates) SRT can capitalized upon identify features variable expression observations different regions tissue section (spatially variable genes SVGs). SVG selection methods often -perform classic single cell nuclei methods generating features recapitulate known spatial domains. However, SVG selection methods permit consideration user-defined batch effects may introduce technical noise sources variation may prevent distort spatial domains.

title: Background
text: analyzing 10X Visium SRT dataset human DLPFC, recognized need evaluate SVGs potential influence batch effects order optimize spatial domain clustering. used nnSVG select 2098 statistically significant SVGs used PRECAST identify clusters hopefully strongly corresponded cortical layers DLPFC. Using K=7 K=8, unable distinguish L5 L6 set features. also identified cluster observations appeared driven low UMI count rather expression SVGs. Figure 1.1 PRECAST clusters: 2098 input features (SVGs), K=7   Figure 1.2 PRECAST clusters: 2098 input features (SVGs), K=8   lack distinction L5 L6 appear due -representation known gene markers cortical layers SVG set. (DLPFC layer markers pulled Huuki-Meyers et. al, 2024 Table S8.) Figure 1.3 SVG set comprises known marker genes DLPFC layers.   Nevertheless, supplemented SVG set significant markers cortical layer (top 100 lowest FDR layer), adding 327 features input clustering. found number L5 L6 markers increased mildly, indicating striking L5 L6 markers already included SVG list. Figure 1.4 Adding significant marker genes marginally increases SVG set.   Supplementing SVGs additional layer markers improve spatial domain clustering PRECAST K=7 K=8. Figure 1.5 PRECAST clusters: 2425 input features (SVGs + layer markers), K=7   Figure 1.6 PRECAST clusters: 2425 input features (SVGs + layer markers), K=8   led us hypothesize small number features within SVG list capturing technical variation gene expression.

title: Background
text: goal use feature selection methods can account batch effects flag SVGs may strongly influenced technical factors. examining per-gene variance/deviance without batch effect, aim identify features interfering generation clusters correspond known DLPFC spatial domains.

title: Find Bias Features - Spatial Transcriptomics Data
text: BiasDetect package developed unrelated DLPFC dataset available spatialLIBD package. first chose binomial deviance model scry feature selection method can incorporate batch variable model. Next, compared per-gene ranks dispersion values model run without batch effect. data-driven thresholding approach using cutoffs based number standard deviance (nSD) deviance rank difference metrics. Based self-selected nSD cutoffs, genes identified biased can filtered . methodology details can found Find Bias Feature written Jacqui Thompson, documentation related codes can found GitHub.

title: Find Bias Features - Spatial Transcriptomics Data
text: BiasDetect R package. Install development version GitHub:

title: Find Bias Features - Spatial Transcriptomics Data
text: Install BiasDetect Load required packages
code: if (!requireNamespace("BiocManager", quietly = TRUE)) {     install.packages("BiocManager") }  BiocManager::install("BiasDetect")  ## Check that you have a valid Bioconductor installation BiocManager::valid() library("BiasDetect") library("SummarizedExperiment") library("SpatialExperiment") library("ggplot2") library("PRECAST") library("scater") library("ggspavis")

title: Find Bias Features - Spatial Transcriptomics Data
text: dataset tutorial section spatialHPC. used nnSVG filter 2082 statistically significant spatially variable genes (SVGs) saved new spe object. filtered new spe object used vignettes .

title: Find Bias Features - Spatial Transcriptomics Data
text: data frame created featureSelect() function includes nSD_dev nSD_rank, can use biasDetect() function identify bias genes based self-selected nSD integer thresholds deviance rank respectively. default threshold 5 deviance 5 rank. ’s , biasDetect function allows return biased genes either data frame format plots. default format data frame. see plots, add visual = TRUE.   Finally, can obtain new spe object removing biased genes.
code: batch_df <- featureSelect(spe_svgs, batch_effect = "sample_id", VGs = SVGs) #> Step 1: Running feature selection without batch... #> Step 2: Running feature selection with batch... #> Step 3: Calculating deviance and rank difference... dim(batch_df) #> [1] 2082   10 head(batch_df) #>              gene gene_name dev_default rank_default dev_batch rank_batch #> 1 ENSG00000002330       BAD    15830.43         1310  15785.12       1289 #> 2 ENSG00000002586      CD99    18837.21          865  18616.10        858 #> 3 ENSG00000004059      ARF5    15033.32         1443  14985.57       1417 #> 4 ENSG00000004660    CAMKK1    14586.54         1506  14479.27       1487 #> 5 ENSG00000004779   NDUFAB1    19859.78          756  19469.72        764 #> 6 ENSG00000005022   SLC25A5    16842.03         1150  16739.61       1131 #>        d_diff     nSD_dev r_diff   nSD_rank #> 1 0.002870288 -0.31052071    -21 -0.4914075 #> 2 0.011877487 -0.13869673     -7 -0.1638025 #> 3 0.003186151 -0.30449522    -26 -0.6084093 #> 4 0.007408683 -0.22394495    -19 -0.4446068 #> 5 0.020034113  0.01690146      8  0.1872029 #> 6 0.006118529 -0.24855631    -19 -0.4446068 bias <- biasDetect(batch_df = batch_df, nSD_dev = 10, nSD_rank = 5) bias #>           GADD45B             DUSP1              EGR1             NR4A1  #> "ENSG00000099860" "ENSG00000120129" "ENSG00000120738" "ENSG00000123358"  #>              FOSB           GADD45G              EGR4              BTG2  #> "ENSG00000125740" "ENSG00000130222" "ENSG00000135625" "ENSG00000159388"  #>               FOS              JUNB             NPAS4               JUN  #> "ENSG00000170345" "ENSG00000171223" "ENSG00000174576" "ENSG00000177606"  #>            HSPA1B              XIST          MTRNR2L1  #> "ENSG00000204388" "ENSG00000229807" "ENSG00000256618" biasDetect(batch_df = batch_df, nSD_dev = 10, nSD_rank = 5, visual = TRUE) #> $deviance #>  #> $rank svgs_filt = setdiff(rownames(spe_svgs), bias) length(svgs_filt) #> [1] 2067

title: Find Bias Features - Spatial Transcriptomics Data
text: can apply PRECAST identify clusters using K = 7. can see, cluster successfully distinguished remove bias genes, proves practicability method.     Please read reference documentation detailed codes scripts conducting PRECAST clustering removing biased gene.
code: path1 <- system.file("extdata","seuInt-hpc_k-7_svgs_metadata.csv", package="BiasDetect") clusters1 <- read.csv(path1, row.names = 1) dim(clusters1) #> [1] 18945     5 identical(rownames(clusters1), rownames(colData(spe_svgs))) #> [1] TRUE spe_svgs$precast_k7 = clusters1$cluster spe_svgs$precast_k7_ordered = factor(     spe_svgs$precast_k7, levels=c(7,2,3,1,6,5,4),      labels=c("WM","WM (2)","SR/SL","CA1","CA3","DG GCL","DG ML"))  path2 <- system.file("extdata","seuInt-hpc_k-7_svgs-no-bias_metadata.csv", package="BiasDetect") clusters2 <- read.csv(path2, row.names = 1) identical(rownames(clusters2), rownames(colData(spe_svgs))) #> [1] TRUE spe_svgs$precast_k7_nobias = clusters2$cluster spe_svgs$precast_k7_nobias_ordered= factor(     spe_svgs$precast_k7_nobias, levels=c(1,2,7,5,6,4,3),     labels=c("WM","SR/SL","CA1","CA1 (2)","CA3","DG GCL","DG ML"))  spe_svgs <- logNormCounts(spe_svgs) markers = c("MBP","GFAP","SPARCL1","FIBCD1","COL5A2","KCNQ5","CARTPT","PCDH8","CALB1") plotGroupedHeatmap(spe_svgs, features = markers, swap_rownames="gene_name",                     group="precast_k7_ordered",                    scale=TRUE, center=TRUE,                     cluster_rows=FALSE, cluster_cols=FALSE) plotGroupedHeatmap(spe_svgs, features = markers, swap_rownames="gene_name",                    group="precast_k7_nobias_ordered",                    scale=TRUE, center=TRUE,                     cluster_rows=FALSE, cluster_cols=FALSE) l2 = unique(spe_svgs$sample_id) names(l2) = l2 l2 = lapply(l2, function(x) spe_svgs[,colData(spe_svgs)$sample_id==x])  col.pal1 = c("#1f77b4FF","#aec7e8FF","#ffbb78FF","#2ca02cFF","#ff7f0eFF","#d62728FF","#ff9896FF") col.pal2 = c("#1f77b4FF","#ffbb78FF","#2ca02cFF","#98df8aFF","#ff7f0eFF","#d62728FF","#ff9896FF")  c1 <- lapply(seq_along(l2), function(x) {   plotSpots(l2[[x]], annotate="precast_k7_ordered")+     labs(color="clus")+     scale_color_manual(values=col.pal1)+     theme(plot.title=element_text(size=8)) }) drawFigs(c1, layout.dim = c(1, 4), common.legend = TRUE, legend.position = "right", align = "h") c2 <- lapply(seq_along(l2), function(x) {   plotSpots(l2[[x]], annotate="precast_k7_nobias_ordered")+     labs(color="clus")+     scale_color_manual(values=col.pal2)+     theme(plot.title=element_text(size=8)) }) drawFigs(c2, layout.dim = c(1, 4), common.legend = TRUE, legend.position = "right", align = "h")

title: Authors and Citation
text: Hou C, Thompson J, Hicks S (2024). BiasDetect: Find Biased Features. R package version 0.99.0, https://github.com/christinehou11/BiasDetect, https://christinehou11.github.io/BiasDetect/.
code: @Manual{,   title = {BiasDetect: Find Biased Features},   author = {Christine Hou and Jacqui Thompson and Stephanie C. Hicks},   year = {2024},   note = {R package version 0.99.0, https://github.com/christinehou11/BiasDetect},   url = {https://christinehou11.github.io/BiasDetect/}, }

title: Find Biased Features
text: BiasDetect feature-based Quality Control (QC) bias genes identification spatial transcriptomics snRNA-seq data types batch effect. BiasDetect method based binomial deviance model (Townes et al, 2019) applies cutoffs based standard deviation (SD) deviance rank difference metrics data-driven thresholding approach find batch-biased features.

title: Find Biased Features
text: BiasDetect R package available Bioconductor version 3.19 later. can install BiasDetect using following commands R session Bioconductor: Additionally, can install development version GitHub: Install additional required packages running package codes vignettes.
code: if (!require("BiocManager", quietly = TRUE))     install.packages("BiocManager")  BiocManager::install("BiasDetect")  ## Check Bioconductor installation BiocManager::valid() BiocManager::install("christinehou11/BiasDetect") pkgs <- c("dplyr", "tidyr", "scran", "scry", "nnSVG") required_pkgs <- pkgs[!pkgs %in% rownames(installed.packages())] BiocManager::install(required_pkgs)

title: Find Biased Features
text: detailed tutorial available Find Biased Features written Jacqui Thompson.

title: Find Bias Genes — biasDetect
text: Function filter bias genes based selected selected threshold nSD deviance rank data frame plot.

title: Find Bias Genes — biasDetect
text: batch_df data.frame : Input data frame generated featureSelection() function using SpatialExperiment SingleCellExperiment object containing raw data. nSD_dev integer: Number standard deviation (nSD) deviance difference. default value 5. nSD_rank integer: Number standard deviation (nSD) rank difference. default value 5. visual logical: Whether display detected bias genes visualizations deviance rank. Default = FALSE return data frame. TRUE, returned format two parallel plots presenting bias genes based nSD_dev nSD_rank.

title: Find Bias Genes — biasDetect
text: output values identified bias genes. returned format can either data frame list two elements, giving option see either deviance rank plot.

title: Feature Selection — featureSelect
text: Function conduct feature selection snRNA-seq spatial transcriptomics data calculate difference deviance rank without selected batch effect.

title: Feature Selection — featureSelect
text: input SpatialExperiment SingleCellExperiment: Input data can either SpatialExperiment SingleCellExperiment. assumed assays slot containing counts assay devianceFeatureSelection() successfully operate calculate deviance rank. logcounts strongly recommended included assays. Also, input data assumed set rownames(input) genes gene_name rowData(input). batch_effect character: Either batch slide subject based metadata available within input data. name slide subject within input object specified based different scenarios. VGs character: Highly Variable Genes (HVGs) SingleCelleExperiment object Spatially Variable Genes (SVGs) SpatialExperiment object. data frame, assumed contain one column identified variable genes column name "gene_name".

title: Feature Selection — featureSelect
text: input provided SpatialExperiment SingleCellExperiment object, output values returned data frame containing deviance rank without bacth effect, corresponding difference, corresponding nSD, gene, gene name, whether gene outlier defined chosen deviance rank cutoff.

title: Feature Selection — featureSelect
code: library(SummarizedExperiment) data(spe) spe_svgs <- spe # the sample dataset here is the SpatialExperiment object containing only # spatially variable genes SVGs <- rowData(spe)$gene_name  set.seed(123) batch_df <- featureSelect(spe_svgs, batch_effect = "sample_id", VGs = SVGs) #> Step 1: Running feature selection without batch... #> Step 2: Running feature selection with batch... #> Step 3: Calculating deviance and rank difference...

title: Human Hippocampus . — spe
text: data generated spatially-resolved transcriptomics (SRT) single-nucleus RNA-sequencing (snRNA-seq) data adjacent tissue sections anterior human hippocampus across ten adult neurotypical donors. SRT data generated using 10x Genomics Visium (n=36 capture areas) 10x Genomics Visium Spatial Proteogenomics (SPG) (n=8 capture areas). snRNA-seq data generated using 10x Genomics Chromium (n=26 total snRNA-seq libraries). original data processed using nnSVG() feature selection, spe dataset SpatialExperiment object containing 2098 spatially variable genes (SVGs).

title: Human Hippocampus . — spe
text: SpatialExperiment object.
